title: High stability of microRNAs in tissue samples of compromised quality
author: Lorena Peiró-Chova
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: This study investigates the stability of microRNAs (miRNAs) in tissue samples of compromised quality, a common challenge in biobanking and molecular pathology. The authors compared three types of breast cancer tissue samples—optimally frozen samples, severely thawed samples, and matched formalin‑fixed paraffin‑embedded (FFPE) tissues—to evaluate RNA integrity and the recovery of specific RNA species. Total RNA and small RNA fractions were analyzed using electrophoretic profiling and quantitative RT‑PCR. Despite extensive degradation observed in thawed and FFPE samples, miRNAs showed remarkably stable detection across all sample types, with consistent Ct values for miR‑21, miR‑125b, and miR‑191. In contrast, longer RNA molecules such as mRNAs exhibited greater vulnerability to degradation, showing marked differences between high‑quality and compromised tissues. The findings demonstrate that miRNAs remain robustly detectable even in degraded biological materials, supporting their usefulness for molecular studies when optimal specimens are unavailable. This work provides valuable guidance for biobank management and highlights the potential of miRNA‑based analyses in retrospective and diagnostic applications.
